content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length N (1≤N≤5⋅103)N \ (1 \le N \le 5 \cdot 10^3)N (1≤N≤5⋅103) and an integer KKK.

You can flip†^\dag† **at most** KKK characters of the string SSS. Find the **minimum** number of inversions‡^\ddag‡ in the final string.

†^\dag†During a flip, 000 changes to 111 and vice-versa.

‡^\ddag‡A pair of indices (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is said to be an inversion if 1≤i<j≤∣S∣1 \le i\lt j \le |S|1≤i<j≤∣S∣ and Si>SjS_i\gt S_jSi​>Sj​.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two positive integers NNN and KKK −-− the length of SSS and the number of flips that you can perform.
The second line of each test contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the minimum number of inversions that you can get.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.
The sum of N2N^2N2 over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1075 \cdot 10^75⋅107.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We can flip S1S_1S1​ using one flip to obtain S=001S = 001S=001 with zero inversions.
Test case 222: We can flip S1S_1S1​ using one flip to obtain S=0010S = 0010S=0010 with one inversion (3,4)(3,4)(3,4). It can be shown that we cannot achieve less than one inversion using at most 111 flip.
